channel.title: aidemo blog
channel.description: Practical, evidence-backed writing on product demo videos and demo automation.

item.title: Accessible tutorial videos: captions, transcripts, audio description
item.description: Captions clear one of seven WCAG media checks. The descriptive transcript that serves a Deaf-blind reader is the same text your search box needs.

item.title: AI demo video generators: what the label actually covers
item.description: Four unrelated tools rank for 'AI demo video generator,' and two never output a video of your product. A mechanism map so you buy the right object.

item.title: AI documentation tools mapped: Guidde, Scribe, Clueso, Supademo
item.description: Five AI documentation tools get sold as one category. They split into text step guides, narrated video, and click-throughs, and each rots differently.

item.title: AI voiceover for demo videos: quality, cost, and workflow
item.description: AI voiceover per minute is settled and cheap. The real questions: which TTS gives you consistency and one-line re-renders, and where a human voice still wins.

item.title: App preview videos: hitting App Store and Play specs without a reshoot
item.description: Apple wants an uploaded H.264 file at 886 x 1920; Google Play wants a YouTube link. The exact cross-store specs, and how to hit them without a reshoot.

item.title: Keeping App Store screenshots current with automation
item.description: Localize into 50 languages and App Store screenshots become hundreds of PNGs. The fastlane pipeline that regenerates them, and when a browser wins.

item.title: Arcade vs Navattic vs Storylane: picking an interactive demo platform
item.description: They rank on the same grids, but Navattic clones your DOM, Arcade stitches screenshots, and Storylane does both. Pick by capture engine, not feature count.

item.title: Automated product demo videos: regenerate, don't re-record
item.description: Four unrelated machines ship under one label. Only one makes 'the product changed' and 'the demo is current' the same event.

item.title: Automating product screenshots for docs and marketing
item.description: The complete stills playbook: one YAML for every screenshot, a pull request that recaptures them on a schedule, and a visual regression test you get for free.

item.title: Autoplay video on a website: the recipe that works everywhere
item.description: Most autoplay bugs are a missing word from one line of markup. Here is the four-attribute set that plays in every browser, and the code for when it still won't.

item.title: B2B vs PLG demo videos: seven axes that change the cut
item.description: The same product needs two demos: one for a self-serve user, one for a buying committee. Seven axes that decide where each cut lands.

item.title: Best frame rate for screen recording: 30 vs 60 fps by content
item.description: Doubling the frame rate does not double the file, and on a static form it costs almost nothing. A content-type table for when 60 fps is actually worth it.

item.title: Best resolution for screen recording: match delivery, not your monitor
item.description: Your monitor is not the resolution to record at. A source-to-delivery table, the bitrate math, and why a 4K capture can make 1080p text worse.

item.title: Best format for screen recording: container vs codec, decided
item.description: The .mp4 in the filename is the box, not the codec inside it, and the codec is what sets the size. Container and codec, separated and decided.

item.title: Camtasia alternatives for software demos and tutorials
item.description: TechSmith retired Camtasia's perpetual license, and the timeline was always the real bill. The alternatives, sorted by who does the editing.

item.title: Cap vs Screen Studio: open-source polish vs the macOS standard
item.description: Both automate the same polish. One is open source and runs anywhere; one is the macOS benchmark. Where Cap wins, where it breaks, and when to pick neither.

item.title: Letting a coding agent make your demo video
item.description: A coding agent can explore your app and write the demo storyboard. Driving the browser live is the one thing it must not do, and here is exactly why.

item.title: Demo video CTAs: one ask, placed where viewers still are
item.description: An end card offering five things converts nobody. The one-ask rule, a developer CTA taxonomy, and the retention math for where the closing ask goes.

item.title: Captions on demo videos: the accessibility case and the numbers
item.description: Most demo views start muted, so the caption is the demo, not the soundtrack: the timing, the formats, and the WCAG rule that decide whether the words land.

item.title: How much does a demo video cost? Four tiers and the hidden recurring bill
item.description: Four production tiers, priced per finished minute, plus the line item every quote leaves off: what it costs to make the same demo again when the UI moves.

item.title: How to start a demo video: the first five seconds
item.description: Viewers decide to leave in the first few seconds. Six opening moves that earn the next fifty, the one intro to retire, and which hook fits muted autoplay.

item.title: Demo video metrics: play rate, retention, and assisted conversions
item.description: Your dashboard hands you "views" for free, and it is the least useful number you own. Read play rate, the retention curve, and assisted conversions instead.

item.title: Ten demo video mistakes, ranked by how many viewers they cost
item.description: Not all demo blunders are equal. The ones that bite in the first five seconds empty the whole room; the ones at the end cost only the stragglers. Ranked.

item.title: Putting a demo video on your landing page without wrecking load time
item.description: A hero video is the heaviest thing on the page and the thing Google times it by. Here is how to ship one so the page still paints in under 2.5 seconds.

item.title: Demo video script template: problem, walkthrough, proof
item.description: Narration runs about 2.5 words a second, so a demo's runtime is a word budget. Grab a copy-paste script skeleton and two worked scripts, counted line by line.

item.title: Demo video software pricing landscape, per finished minute
item.description: Sticker prices lie because the units differ: per seat, per minute, per demo, per view. Normalized to cost per finished demo-minute, the ranking flips.

item.title: The demo video thumbnail: poster frame vs marketing cover
item.description: The poster that fills the box, the cover that earns the click, and the share card a crawler reads are three different pictures. Which frame to pick for each.

item.title: Rendering demo videos in CI: a GitHub Actions walkthrough
item.description: GitHub's runner ships Chrome but not ffmpeg. Here is what a video-rendering job actually needs, how to trigger it, and where the file should land.

item.title: Demos as code: commit the storyboard, render the video
item.description: A recording is a performance you own and maintain. A spec is text you can diff, review, regenerate, and hand to an agent. That decides everything downstream.

item.title: Descript alternatives for editing demo videos
item.description: Descript edits video by editing the transcript. That trick shines on a podcast and fights a screen demo. The alternatives, sorted by what you grab.
